useful__otherclasses:
Psych1 does a really good job at
  previewing course content for other classes. Almost every psych class I've
  taken after psych1 (if not all) brought up a topic that was touched upon on
  psych1. The classes elaborate upon what I was introduced to in psych1.
useful_life:
I try to always keep in mind that when a
  person is acting rude or is angry, it's not necessarily because they are
  inherently moody. Their bad attitude could be attributed to situational
  factors. This helps me be a less judgmental person and helps me respond more
  rationally.

useful__otherclasses:
I learned that you do not necessarily
  remember happiness in its current state. I make a lot of my decision now on
  what will make me happy later on
useful_life:
Our memory is flawed and we can
  mis-remember events when primed (I was one of the students who falsely
  recalled and described an incident [Prof. [Instructor Name] spilling water on
  himself] that never happened in lecture; my description made it to the memory
  lecture's slides); being more cognizant when I recall events and trying to
  preface more by saying "I think this happened." Activities/physical
  demonstrations of material are highly effective for student engagement and
  later recall of the material (e.g. speed of action potentials in myenelated
  vs. non-myelinated neurons); good to know when facilitating an exercise of
  teaching a lesson. There can always be more "digging" done to
  understand why someone behaved the way they did (i.e. looking into the
  situation as opposed to merely making a dispositional judgment); I like to
  think of this as more progressive lines of thinking that foster increased
  empathy for others. We can continuously improve ourselves when we hold a
  growth mindset towards intelligence and learning. Physiological responses can
  have an influence on our interpretation of emotional responses (i.e.
  suspension bridge misattribution of arousal study); I've had my share of
  instances where I questioned whether I am attracted to this person because I
  genuinely found them attractive or because the hormones released from dancing
  are affecting me. How we viewed the last part of an experience has a strong
  influence on our interpretation of the experience as a whole (e.g. rectal
  exam study); I know that I may be in a bad mood after a fun event simply
  because the last bit turned sour, and vice versa. Lastly, I know what
  extremely high-quality teaching looks like--thank you Prof. [Instructor Name]
  and Prof. Hard!

useful__otherclasses:
Learning to take notes and use them to
  study. Overview of psych topics, and what I was interested in - influenced
  future class selections.
useful_life:
Made some new friends in class! Happiness
  lecture - how things/achievements don't make us happy, but the process of
  meeting our goals does!

useful__otherclasses:
The thought processes that are so often
  encourage and cultivated in Psych classes (to question, investigate, be open
  to new information/outcomes, challenge your own perspective, understanding
  that humans are not always rational and sometimes act based on
  emotion/motives/etc.) The perspective taking and problem solving that we so
  often practice in Psych classes also lends itself well to all studies and
  circumstances, both in the classroom as well as in the workplace, or even in
  personal life.
useful_life:
Well, I spoke to this in my previous
  response, but I'll touch on it once again. The perspective taking and problem
  solving lends itself well to all circumstances (work, personal, academic). I
  think that the openness, curiosity, and adaptable mindset that is so often
  encouraged in Psych classes is also very valuable. Additionally, I think that
  Psych classes generally do a good job of instilling an appreciation for
  people, as well as a desire to understand them better (which is, once again,
  helpful in all areas of life.)
